analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 7 finding(s) from 10 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Bootrom is fully writable by any bus master targeting the ROM address range (0x0001_0000). This allows modification of boot code and device tree, defeating secure boot.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / bootrom memory array (mem[]) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any AXI bus master (CPU via slave[0], debug master via slave[1]) Operation: write Object: bootrom memory array (mem[]) Expected guard: we_i permanently tied to 0 or write access disabled by a lock bit or privilege check Observed behavior: mem[] updated unconditionally when req_i and we_i are both asserted, per bootrom.sv:258-265; no lock, no privilege gating Path: AXI master → axi_node → master[ROM] → axi2mem (ariane_testharness.sv:228-252) → rom_we → i_bootrom.we_i Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Boot code corruption, device‑tree tampering, complete secure‑boot bypass
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: None; bootrom source clearly shows writable array with no write-protection

findings.summary: AES cryptographic key and intermediate state are readable by any bus master via the APB interface. No privilege or lock mechanism protects these registers.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / AES key_big[191:0], inter_state[127:0], ct[127:0], p_c[127:0] / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master (CPU in any mode, debug master, DMA) that can read the AES APB address range Operation: read Object: AES key_big[191:0], inter_state[127:0], ct[127:0], p_c[127:0] Expected guard: Reads returning key or intermediate state must be restricted to machine‑mode or completely disabled Observed behavior: aes_wrapper.sv:129-147 returns key_big and inter_state slices unconditionally based on address decode; no privilege check in the read mux or in the preceding APB bridge Path: AXI → axi2apb → i_apb_to_reg_aes → reg_bus_aes → aes_wrapper.external_bus_io.rdata (lines 109‑149) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Full extraction of the 192‑bit AES key and 128‑bit intermediate state, compromising all past and future AES operations
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: None; read path is fully visible and contains no privilege filtering

findings.summary: AES control and plaintext/state registers are writable by any bus master, allowing chosen‑plaintext attacks and unauthorized AES operation.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / AES start, p_c[3:0], state[3:0] registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master that can write the AES APB address range Operation: write Object: AES start, p_c[3:0], state[3:0] registers Expected guard: Writes to these registers must be restricted to machine‑mode or require a lock release Observed behavior: aes_wrapper.sv:75‑98 updates start, p_c, state unconditionally on external_bus_io.write; no privilege gating Path: AXI → axi2apb → i_apb_to_reg_aes → reg_bus_aes → aes_wrapper write case (lines 75‑98) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthorised injection of chosen plaintext, arbitrary initiation of AES operations, undermining cryptographic guarantees
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: None; all write‑path RTL is visible and lacks any privilege check

findings.summary: The debug master has unrestricted AXI bus access with no effective authentication, allowing an attacker with JTAG access to read/write any peripheral including AES keys and bootrom.
findings.vulnerability_category: read, write / All address‑mapped resources (bootrom, rom2, AES registers, etc.) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Debug master (i_axi_master_dm) on slave[1] Operation: read, write Object: All address‑mapped resources (bootrom, rom2, AES registers, etc.) Expected guard: Debug master must be disabled or require successful JTAG authentication (e.g., via jtag_key) before bus access; bus fabric should deny debug access to protected regions Observed behavior: Debug master connected to slave[1] (ariane_testharness.sv:215) giving full AXI bus access; no authentication gating visible before bus transactions; access_ctrl from rom2 (writable) can potentially allow debug access to all peripherals Path: JTAG → dmi_jtag → dm_top → axi_m_req_o → i_axi_master_dm → slave[1] → axi_node → masters[N] Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debug interface provides complete bypass of all software‑enforced protections, exposing keys, boot code, and cryptographic state
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal logic of dmi_jtag is a black box; exact authentication sequence is unknown, but the fact that jtag_key comes from writable rom2 undermines any potential protection there.

findings.summary: rom2 secure_reg (containing critical keys and configuration) is writable by any bus master, allowing an attacker to replace keys and access control policies.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / rom2 secure_reg (contains JTAG key, AES key, access control values) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any bus master targeting ROM2 address range (0x0021_0000) Operation: write Object: rom2 secure_reg (contains JTAG key, AES key, access control values) Expected guard: rom2 must be read‑only after provisioning; we_i tied to 0 or locked by sticky bit Observed behavior: rom2.sv:37‑39 writes wdata_i to secure_reg[addr] when req_i && we_i; no lock bit or protection Path: AXI → master[ROM2] → axi2mem → rom2_we → i_rom2.we_i → secure_reg write Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Attacker can overwrite AES key, JTAG key, and access control registers, completely breaking system security
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: None; rom2 secure_reg is clearly writable

findings.summary: AES ciphertext and intermediate state are not cleared on reset, creating a state residue vulnerability that leaks previous cryptographic results.
findings.vulnerability_category: read after reset / AES ct and inter_state registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Post‑reset reader (any bus master reading AES registers after a system reset) Operation: read after reset Object: AES ct and inter_state registers Expected guard: Reset logic must clear all security‑sensitive state (including ct and inter_state) to zero Observed behavior: aes_wrapper.sv:65‑73 only clears p_c[3:0] and state[3:0] on reset; no reset assignments for ct, inter_state, or ct_valid. These registers retain their previous values across reset. Path: rst_ni → always block (only p_c, state, start cleared); ct and inter_state derive from the AES core, which is not shown to have guaranteed reset clearing Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Information leakage of previous cryptographic computations across reset boundaries
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal reset of the AES core (aes_192_sed) is unknown, but the wrapper's own registers ct and inter_state are not cleared; the ciphertext path from the core to the read mux may retain data.

findings.summary: JTAG key is stored in writable rom2, allowing attackers to read or replace it; combined with a short key length and lack of visible anti‑hammering, debug unlock protection is easily bypassed.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/modify jtag_key or brute‑force debug unlock / jtag_key (32‑bit value used for debug authentication) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Software attacker or physical attacker with bus access Operation: read/modify jtag_key or brute‑force debug unlock Object: jtag_key (32‑bit value used for debug authentication) Expected guard: Key must be non‑readable by software; debug module must implement anti‑hammering Observed behavior: jtag_key is derived from rom2 secure_reg[1][31:0] (ariane_peripherals.sv:215); rom2 is writable (see rom2_write_allowed), so the key can be read back and overwritten. No anti‑hammering logic is visible in the testharness or the black‑box dmi_jtag instance; the key length is only 32 bits, making brute‑force feasible. Path: rom2 secure_reg → ariane_peripherals jtag_key output → ariane_testharness jtag_key input → dmi_jtag (black box) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Attacker can extract or overwrite the JTAG key, unlocking debug access and compromising all system assets
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal authentication logic of dmi_jtag and SimDTM is not available; however, because the key source (rom2) is writable, the protection is fundamentally broken regardless of the black‑box implementation.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
